sentences.text: Our rav was such a distinguished man, really handsome man, and his son.
Believe me, I don't remember.
I'm so bad at remembering names.
I remember more faces, normally, than names.
Very bad at remembering names.
Well, on that day everybody-- they were brought into the center and they-- everybody had to come to the center, everybody, all the Jews.
I think even non Jews.
Everybody had to come to the center.
The Germans made like a pantomime out of it.
They had music.
They had everything.
And these poor men were being hung.
It was a terrible blow, terrible, terrible things happening.
Well, they would pick.
They would choose from the private people.
Each one would hang the other.
I mean, it's so mean to say that the 12 people who were chosen to be hung were hung by Jewish people, who were chosen by the Germans who should hang, these people who were chosen to be hung.
No justification.
They just took the Ruv and the four for hanging, and all the prominent people.
No.
And in actual fact, my uncle, which was the younger brother of my father, he was the richest Jew in town.
From the Munters.
I mean, he was a very capable man.
And he was also very clever.
I mean, he bought up a whole village.
And lots of farmers were on this village, workers, farm workers.
And the first thing, when they came to our town, is they asked about the rabbi, the ruv, and the richest Jew.
And the richest Jew was my uncle.
So he had great problems, terrible problems.
First of all, he was also chosen to be hung.
But because I was working for this meister-- my sister afterwards came to work also to help at the meister's.
She was doing mainly cleaning and washing up dishes and so on.
She asked me if I have got a sister who would come and do the help.
So that was my sister.
And what was once again other-- we don't know how.
I know that we had to give them a lot of money for it.
But it didn't matter, gold and [INAUDIBLE] were.
And somehow or other, the only one who was being taken off this was my uncle.
But it didn't help.
In 1942 he was sent with all the rest to be killed.
No, I don't remember that.
I remember it was a summer day.
I don't know when it was, whether it was summer or it was spring.
I can't remember exactly.
But it was a blow.
My father was always hiding.
He never came out.
He was always in the cellar.
He was always hiding.
He didn't want to see anybody and so on.
But there was one time where, actually, I managed to get him a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] bescheinigung, what is that? a-- --certificate.
Certificate.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I managed to get him a certificate that he could go and work for the burgermeister.
This happened because when the burgermeister, at the time when he said I'm a guter arbeiter.
And I was walking with my sister-- with my mother-- one day.
And my mother said, you know, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] if my father could get outside the ghetto somehow, we could get food.
Because we had the money.
We could buy butter.
I mean, around everywhere there were still villages and so on.
So one day she said I should go to speak to the burgermeister because at one time he was caught-- my father was caught when they were catching men to work.
And I got through, however, through this town, this small town to get as far as the magistrate.
And I said to the-- I was very courageous at that time.
But I did it because I had to do it.
It wasn't only courage.
I felt I had to do it.
I ran down the stairs.
And I looked when the burgermeister was turning this way.
I could see him.
I ran down the stairs.
When he saw me, he was so surprised.
What am I doing here?
They were looking-- they needed men for work.
And many men have hidden.
So they didn't have enough people.
But they knew exactly how many there were in the town.
But so many had hidden.
So he got very cross and he started shouting in German, [GERMAN]!
And at that moment, I wanted to take my father out, which I-- well, anyway, I got down.
At least, I looked through the keyhole to see if he sees me.
Because if not, they could kill me, the others who were around that magistrate.
And when he saw me he was so surprised.
He came over, he says, [GERMAN]?
And I said, Herr Burgermeister.
And I couldn't speak.
I was so emotional.
I was crying.
I couldn't get the word out.
So he says, [GERMAN].
And I said, my father, my father, my father.
That's all I could say, my father.
He says, [GERMAN]?
And I showed him where he is with all the other people being caught.
And I said to him, my father is an older man.
I especially said, you know that he is not so young.
Anyway, [GERMAN]?
Munter.
Ah, Munter.
He remembered Munter.
So he went down.
And when he went down, my father got such a shock because he called out Munter.
And my father didn't know what happened.
But then he saw me.
So he could see that I was actually trying to do something.
Sure enough, he started screaming at my father.
And he said, what are you doing here?
Why don't you work in my garden?
Because he couldn't just take him out like that.
I mean, there had to be a reason.
And when he did that, my father came out, and we went into the ghetto.
The both of us came into the ghetto from the center.
We went from the magistrate into the ghetto.
And this was when I arrived, everybody wanted me I should take out the other people, but I couldn't.
I couldn't.
People wanted to give me [INAUDIBLE] what I should go and take people out.
But how could I?
But, anyway, when I came back with my father, about three, four, five weeks later, my mother kept saying maybe you'll be able to get-- you can see he is very favorable to you.
Maybe you could get him a certificate.
And so one day when he was standing here.
He was always standing somewhere or other watching the Jewish people working hard.
Because they always caught them to do this work or that one.
My mother said, go and ask him.
Maybe you can do it.
And I was stubborn.
When my mother wanted me to do it I didn't want to go.
But if I'm alone, I was running like mad, which was very dangerous.
But in the end I went.
I went to the burgermeister.
And I said thank you very much.
I said danke, Herr Burgermeister, that he took my father out.
And now I said my father would very much like to work at his garden, if it's possible.
And my father's-- ja, jawohl, why not?
You know?
He can do the work.
Because they always were getting people to work.
But, anyway, I said my father cannot go outside into the ghetto unless he's allowed if he's got a certificate.
He said [GERMAN].
I will give you that certificate.
And sure enough I said, whether I can come Monday at this and this time.
He said, yes.
Anyway, when I came down through the center, I told everybody in the workshop that I'd be walking down to the magistrate in the ghetto-- outside the ghetto.
And they couldn't believe it when they saw me.
I said when I come back with a piece of paper I'll wave it to you.
I went down.
When I came in they had the secretary there, Polish girl.
She was a great anti-Semite.
She always was very anti-Semitic.
And when she saw me coming she said what are you doing here?
To me in Polish.
I said I have got an appointment with the Herr Burgermeister.
So she was really plotzing.
She said-- so she called to Herr Burgermeister.
And when he came in I said, Herr Burgermeister, certificate for my father?
You know, for my father?
So he made her type, the secretary.
She was typing out.
And I had this certificate.
And then I came back , and I showed everybody I got the certificate.
My father had it all right.
But when they came, when they rounded us all up in 1942, and he was holding the certificate in his hand.
And the Gestapo took it in front of the burgermeister, took the paper, tore it.
Shyster, you know they said, you know?
What did the certificate say?
It said that he had that Munter, Jacob Munter, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] is allowed-- is going to work in the gardens for the magistrate.
It was a thing.
I mean, my father didn't get paid or anything.
But thank God, he could get out of the ghetto.
Because this way we could get some butter.
We could then get some eggs and things, food, you know.
Till 1942.
And in '42 they rounded us up.
I was working with the tailors already at the time.
There was another instance that was right in the beginning as well, when the burgermeister came with the secretary.
They were going from place to place to take the inventory, what everyone owns, Jewish people.
And especially people who owned land.
And they came around to our house.
And I could see through the kitchen window him going down the steps to the yard down there with the secretary.
And my mother was alone with me.
My father was hiding in the cellar.
He wouldn't come out at all.
My father had a big beard.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my sisters were out.
So it was only me and my mother.
So my mother was starting like this, when she asked the burgermeister here, what do they want?
So I said, Mama, tell me exactly what we have got.
What should I tell them?
Because I didn't know.
And I will go down.
She said, no, how can you?
You're only a child.
I said, Mama, just tell me what.
I will go down.
When I ran down the stairs to come down, again, this one [INAUDIBLE] said to me, [GERMAN]?
I said, I will tell everything what you want to know.
Just ask me, and I will tell you what.
And the burgermeister seeing a young girl like that coming down.
He started laughing.
He said, ho, ho, ho.
He started laughing in his big voice like that.
And he made a joke as well, whether I can milk cows.
I never even went near a cow.
I was afraid of cows.
I said, yes.
I was never going to show I'm going to be beaten by them.
I was very courageous.
But God forbid, if he asked me to do it I would have been in trouble.
Because I've never done it in my life.
And this was the time when I actually was called afterwards to work.
When I was doing the clearing of the rubble, he actually also recognized me.
Because he saw me first of all at home.
He already new my name.
He already asked me my name.
Now he asked me again my name.
And he remembered.
And he said, [GERMAN].
No.
They only came that once.
And I told them exactly.
And that was that.
Well, they remained in the house.
We didn't-- four in the morning they-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we were driven out of the house.
Raus!
Raus!
Nothing.
I wouldn't know anything what happened afterwards.
Yes, they remained.
So I don't know--
There are people living in our house.
The house is still there.
I've never even gone back or inquired or did anything about it.
Because at first there was the communism there.
And then afterwards, I don't know.
I never-- I have got really no will even to go there or to see it.
No, no nothing.
Nothing.
We were away from that place.
They probably moved in and had a nice place to live in.
And we lived in this-- we couldn't go there anyway.
They were outside ghetto.
We were in the ghetto.
Who could go to see them?
I didn't anyway.
I couldn't.
When?
We lived our life.
It was not a very nice life, you can imagine, in the ghetto.
But my father, being a religious man, even in the ghetto he had to have a sukkah.
So he dug out together with other Jews, they dug out a very big hole, like a big-- they put a leather and covered it up on the top with, you know from potatoes.
When the potatoes grow, these things they put on first the wood, and this is from the potato.
And were terrified if the Germans were coming through, and one of them would go through this sukkah.
They would fall into the sukkah.
We would have been in trouble.
They would have seen what it is.
Because inside we all made it nice like a real sukkah with covers and everything.
But my father wouldn't eat unless he-- even when so long we were at home, he would have to eat in the sukkah.
No, my father.
We didn't go down to that hole down there, no.
I mean, big dug out in the garden a thing.
My father, we brought down the food for him and for some other men.
Anyway, we were going to the ghetto.
And in the ghetto, as I said, when we arrived it was a terrible shock to see these children.
That was Lodz ghetto.
They were so starved.
And it was terrible.
And then, of course, I was in the ghetto with my-- the sad day my other sister came.
On the second day my other sister-- because there were three of us.
When we arrived, I said to you that we arrived into the ghetto in the poorest part, where there was a prison.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we saw the children being imprisoned just because they stole.
Nobody in the ghetto was stealing any material things.
Nobody wanted anything.
Jewelry was-- nobody wanted to have it.
Because if you had a ring or you had anything, you would be taken into the Kripo, to the German Criminal Police.
And they would always want more.
And in any case, you couldn't-- nobody-- if somebody stole, they stole food.
They never stole anything of material, not in the ghetto.
So, in fact, it was several times, we were even stolen of our ration, which that was a terrible disaster.
Because the ration was so little.
You couldn't survive on the ration in the ghetto.
It was very difficult.
The main thing was to survive.
Now everybody had to work.
When we arrived in the ghetto, we were all three living together.
And one of my sisters made a soup in the evening from the ration.
So she had to divide to make sure that there is enough.
And if you had a half a potato for the evening in the soup, and the flour was just like salt.
You put it now like that.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But you had to live that way.
Because otherwise you couldn't-- you couldn't survive like that.
But you could even survive less if you didn't divide it every evening to have that little bit.
Because it was mainly hot water with that little piece of potato and a bit of flour.
And it was terrible.
They gave us a loaf of bread for a week, which you had to divide every day a piece.
But you had no butter.
You had no cheese.
You had no tomatoes.
You had not fruit.
You had nothing.
You didn't see any eggs.
You had nothing.
You had no salami or anything like that.
So you can imagine that people were just dying like flies in the street in the ghetto.
I got very, very ill in the Lodz ghetto.
I got tuberculosis.
I was getting thinner and thinner.
And still I had to go to work.
If I didn't go to work, I would miss the soup lunchtime.
Because we had nothing from morning till the evening, unless you were working in the factory.
You had the soup.
And that soup, if you were lucky, you had a half a potato.
And if you were not lucky, you didn't.
It was all like that.
But still you had to go.
You had to work.
In any case, if you didn't work, you were in danger to be sent to Auschwitz straight away, see.
So you had to work.
I was sent to a military [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] factory where they did all for the military, all those bags here which they have-- all those things where they keep all the ammunition.
It was [GERMAN].
That means it was made from leather.
And also from the material, you know, which was the canvass material.
That was-- it was run by the Jewish people.
But it was in the ghetto.
Everything which was in the ghetto was for the Germans.
In the beginning I was doing all kinds, carrying this, bringing this to the machines, doing that.
Then afterwards they put me on a machine.
And because I was tall I could reach, just about, the pedal.
Because they were very heavy machines.
And it was very hard work.
Because every day the factory director or manager would come around and say, we need more.
He always wanted more.
They wanted more production and no food.
And they were rushing us like mad.
And God forbid if you made a mistake.
Everything had to be stitched right.
And you had to really get yourself adapted to do the job properly.
I mean, it was very hard.
It was strenuous, worrying, hard, and the starvation.
And I was ill too.
And I had to do it.
Oh, nobody would.
If you made a mistake it was terrible.
You were not allowed to make mistakes.
Well, they chose men to run the factory.
Usually the men who run the factory were not in a good position.
Because they were responsible for the work to be done properly and on time and everything.
And they didn't have any more food than anybody else.
The only ones who could survive easy were the ones who worked where the food arrived, like the potatoes and cabbage, whatever there was.
Then you could have-- they would be watched very much.
But still it was easier to work at that than to run a factory.
This was a responsible job.
And every time they had to produce more and more.
It was very difficult.
No, in the ghetto, only Jewish people.
We were--
The whole ghetto was only Jewish.
Only when the Germans came in, they only came to look and ran out.
That wasn't a place for them to be.
It was a horrible place to be in.
It was lots of illness, and lots of dysentery, and other illnesses.
And people were dying in the street.
It was a terrible thing.
They had one alteste der Juden, which was the top.
He was running the whole ghetto.
He had a little horse, a horse and a cart.
And he was going around the whole ghetto.
And he was being treated better.
And every time they wanted more from him.
He was, before the war, quite a nice man.
Came from a good family and everything.
And then, slowly, slowly, they wanted more children from him.
They wanted more this, always more people.
It was very difficult for him.
His name was Rumkowski, and he was very well known in the ghetto.
And, amazingly, the ghetto was run in such a way that they had police.
The police was wearing a hat with a band around it.
Cardinal red, it had.
The doctors also had a band around it.
But they had no medicine.
They had nothing.
And everybody was so ill.
It was run like a Jewish little state, but without food, and with hard work, and under pressure from the Germans.
It was very, very difficult.
In 1944-- till 1944 we were in the ghetto.
Not only that, where we lived, to get from one side to the ghetto to the other, because Lodz is quite a big town-- and this was the worst part of Lodz anyway.
It was like the east end of London.
Near our house was a big bridge.
They built these enormous bridges to go across, to be able to get-- because imagine there is a road.
The road was run outside by the Germans.
Everybody was using it, the Poles, the Germans.
And we were closed in by fence.
And every, I don't know, every meter or every one and a half meter was one of the Germans with a rifle.
It was guarded all the time.
They were always changing the guards there.
And always, day and night, it's being guarded, the ghetto, so you couldn't get out.
So to get across the other side, from one side to the other, we had to cross a bridge.
And in winter the bridges got so snowed on and iced up.
And It was impossible to get across.
You had to have the strength to get across to the other side.

And then I had that really hard life because from there I had to cross another, not a bridge, this time gates where the Germans were standing and wouldn't let you go across until there was about 200 people standing in the cold and the ice in the morning.
And then we had to arrive on time to the factory.
Otherwise we didn't get the soup.
We couldn't get in because they wouldn't let you in late in the factory.
It was terribly difficult.
But there are two fellows who were with me in the ghetto, in the same factory.
And every time when I came in, we had a thing to give up when we came in in the factory, out of card [INAUDIBLE].
So I used to throw it across, and they opened the gate for me and let me in.
They felt sorry for me.
My sister worked in a shoe factory.
Slippers-- they used to-- you know, from Auschwitz, from the clothes, which used to come back, they used to make [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] slippers out of it.
But there was a way.
What they done is, you know when you cut your hair, a [GERMAN].
I forgot now what it's called.
[GERMAN], when you use--
A plait.
They make these plaits out of the material from the people who died in Auschwitz.
All these clothes, where all this was coming to the ghetto, they made plaits.
And this was all very colorful because it was from most summer material and so from the dresses.
And they made slippers out of it.
My sister worked in a slipper factory.
And she was, in fact, good.
She was talented.
Because they gave her to make the little things, the model.
The model of the slipper, she used to make.
My other sister worked-- I can't remember where she worked, because she wasn't very long in the ghetto.
She was standing in a queue.
They were telling us there are queues for bread.
And so my sister went down.
Actually, I went first to this queue.
But my sister wouldn't let me stand too long in the queue.
So she sent me up.
She says, you go up.
Because I was so ill.
And she was standing in the queue.
And when it was getting dusk, and when she didn't come back, we went to look.
There was no queue.
They had taken all the people and sent them to Auschwitz.
So she wasn't very long in Auschwitz.
I remained with my sister.
We have to change tapes.
